payload.answer: Store the dataset as structured tabular records with explicit columns (id, name, value). Optionally index by id to support fast lookup; if id is not unique, avoid enforcing uniqueness and store rows as-is or use a composite uniqueness policy (id,name,value).
payload.decision: Store it as a structured, queryable tabular record set (e.g., relational table or document-store collection) with a dedicated schema for columns (id, name, value) rather than as an unstructured text blob; optionally add indexing on id for deduplication and fast lookup.

entity_resolution.reason: No explicit definition is provided for whether id is unique or a primary key, so entity resolution for keying/indexing cannot be finalized.

payload.intent: Determine an appropriate storage design for a CSV table with 500 rows (id,name,value repeated many times) in a personal memory system.

memory_cards.statement: Fixture large_table_500_rows_001 contains a CSV table with repeated rows/records using columns id, name, and value.
For queryability and scalability, store CSV row data as structured records with explicit fields (id, name, value) instead of a single concatenated text representation.

If id is intended to be unique (or used for lookup), consider indexing or enforcing deduplication by id; if uniqueness is not guaranteed, treat (id,name,value) as composite uniqueness or store all rows as-is.

details: Extracted salient schema elements (id,name,value) and recommended a structured storage approach with conditional keying based on id uniqueness.

repair_options: Verify whether id is unique; if yes, enforce primary-key semantics and deduplicate on insert.
If id is not unique, define a composite key policy (e.g., id+name+value) or allow duplicates intentionally.
Decide whether name/value should be normalized into separate reference tables or kept denormalized in one table.
